channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: This Star Just Ate a Planet, and It’s Not Done Yet
item.description: A star 1,300 light-years away appears to have just consumed one world and is gearing up for seconds.
item.dc:creator: Becky Ferreira

item.title: Nobel-Winning U.S. Chemist Omar Yaghi Will Move to China to Lead A.I. Institute
item.description: Omar Yaghi of the University of California, Berkeley, will head an initiative to apply artificial intelligence to the discovery of new materials.
item.dc:creator: William J. Broad
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Omar Yaghi shared last year’s Nobel Prize in Chemistry for helping develop frameworks of chemical building blocks with vast internal surface areas, which he named metal-organic frameworks.

item.title: These Fossils May Be the Earliest Evidence of Handedness in Animals
item.description: Scientists propose that recently uncovered fossils may be the earliest evidence of behavioral “handedness” in animals.
item.dc:creator: Jack Tamisiea

item.title: Wally Funk, Who Set an Age Record for Space Travel, Dies at 87
item.description: As a young woman in the 1960s, she wasn’t allowed to become a NASA astronaut. She finally realized her long-held dream of flying in space as an octogenarian.
item.dc:creator: Richard Goldstein
item.media:description: Wally Funk in 2021, after returning from a roughly 10-minute spaceflight aboard a rocket built by the company Blue Origin. “I loved every minute of it,” she said. “I just wish it had been longer.”

item.title: A Chinese Spacecraft Captures First Image of Quasi-Moon
item.description: A Chinese spacecraft has captured the first image of the asteroid Kamo‘oalewa.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews

item.title: Spread of Seafloor Directly Observed for the First Time
item.description: The spread of the ocean floor, as tectonic plates spread apart, is known but hard to observe. Scientists have now documented the process in action.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway
item.media:description: Île Saint-Paul, a tiny island in the Indian Ocean, some 120 miles southwest of the deep sea site where the Antarctica and Australia tectonic plates diverged during an earthquake in 2024.

item.title: Chernobyl’s Wildlife Cameras Reveal How War Affects Wild Animals
item.description: A rare camera-trap study logged the effects of armed conflict on wild animals in real time.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: Frankfurt Zoological Society, Chernobyl Radiation and Ecological Biosphere Reserve/Wild Polissia Program
item.media:description: Researchers found few behavioral changes in lynxes, whereas different species of deer changed their activity patterns during the Russian occupation.

item.title: As Ice Melts in the Arctic, Some Deep-Sea Creatures Are Thriving
item.description: A new study suggests that deep-sea life reaps the benefits of icebergs’ castoffs — a rare silver lining as a warming planet destabilizes glacial ice.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway

item.title: Cosmic Conjoined Twins, Caught on Camera
item.description: A Japanese spacecraft flew by an asteroid and snapped a picture. It turned out to be a “contact binary”: two space rocks that somehow fused together.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews
item.media:description: Torifune, an asteroid known as a “contact binary,” observed by the Japanese spacecraft Hayabusa2 on July 5.

item.title: Advanced Recycling Hits a Rough Patch
item.description: In the past weeks, two sites in the United States have halted operations.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Unlike traditional mechanical recycling, advanced recycling can theoretically process mixed or contaminated plastics that otherwise would end up in landfills.

item.title: Trump’s Plans for Nuclear Power Lurch Ahead
item.description: Recent technological advances have brought the president’s goals a bit closer to reality.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:description: Energy Secretary Chris Wright, center, visited the National Reactor Innovation Center near Idaho Falls, Idaho, last month.

item.title: I.U.C.N. Update Says Deep-Sea Mining Threatens Mollusks Around Hydrothermal Vents
item.description: The snails and other mollusks around hydrothermal vents have evolved to thrive in extreme conditions, but mineral extraction could drive more than half to extinction.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.category: International Union for Conservation of Nature

item.title: How to Turn Your Phone Into a Personal Health Dashboard
item.description: Free apps from Google, Samsung and Apple can help you track your diet, exercise and well-being — and provide vital information during emergencies.
item.dc:creator: J. D. Biersdorfer
item.media:description: From left, the Google Health, the Samsung Health and Apple’s Health apps offer plenty of tools and features to help you manage your diet, exercise and personal safety.

item.title: This Kind of Obsessive Attraction Isn’t Love. But It Has a Name.
item.description: Limerence is more than a crush, psychologists say, and it can persist for months or years.
item.dc:creator: Melinda Wenner Moyer

item.title: Western Europe Had Its Hottest June on Record
item.description: An unusually early heat wave smashed records across France, Britain and Spain. Temperatures in some areas are starting to soar again.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:description: Riding a hot train in London on June 24. Average temperatures across Western Europe last month were 3.05 degrees Celsius, or 5.5 degrees Fahrenheit, above the mean from recent decades.

item.title: Global Warming Could Ravage the Amazon’s Useful Plant Species, Study Finds
item.description: Hotter temperatures and harsher droughts could cause Indigenous societies to lose many of the species they have used for medicine, rituals and more, scientists found.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Flávia Milhorance
item.media:credit: Victor Moriyama for The New York Times
item.media:description: The study warned that major losses of plant species could occur in the coming decades even if nations limit global warming to moderate levels.

item.title: Our Bacteria Are Talking. We’ve Just Begun to Understand What They’re Saying.
item.description: The human microbiome is essential to our health, but scientists still know very little about it. Two researchers set out to map this largely uncharted terrain.
item.dc:creator: Jeneen Interlandi

item.title: New Rules Require Election Changes for States to Get Terrorism Grants
item.description: Federal officials said they would withhold some money unless states pursue paper ballot systems, verify citizenship and conduct costly audits.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance, Nick Corasaniti and Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times
item.media:description: Courts have already blocked similar efforts by the administration to withhold funding, saying the Constitution does not grant the executive branch power over elections.

item.title: Nation’s 250th Celebration Fireworks Sent D.C. Air Pollution Spiking
item.description: The president promised a record display of some 850,000 pyrotechnics for the country’s 250th birthday. The Park Service warned it could get dirty.
item.dc:creator: Quinn Glabicki
item.media:description: The pollution began dissipating by morning, though it lingered longer in neighborhoods to the north of the city.

item.title: I Wanted an Ecologically Responsible Garden. It Was Harder Than I Thought.
item.description: The native plant movement gets a lot right, but there’s so much more to consider.
item.dc:creator: Ferris Jabr
item.media:credit: Balarama Heller for The New York Times
item.media:description: A field of Phacelia blooming on the property of Douglas Tallamy.

item.title: Mysterious Spheres Found in Australia Are Likely Space Debris
item.description: The discovery bemused residents of a small town in northeastern Australia. The objects appear to be junk from a space launch, the country’s space agency said.
item.dc:creator: Hari Raj

item.title: Heat Waves Are Taking a Big Toll on Chickens
item.description: The birds are particularly vulnerable to high temperatures, and France’s torrid June took a heavy toll.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan and Léontine Gallois
item.media:credit: Samuel Aranda for The New York Times

item.title: Bill Frist Wants to Take Politics Out of Climate Change
item.description: Bill Frist, a surgeon who also served as Senate majority leader, has emerged as an outspoken advocate for environmental health. “A healthier planet means healthier people,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Cara Buckley
item.media:credit: Kirsten Luce for The New York Times

item.title: Efforts to Help Smokers Quit Stall Under Trump
item.description: The C.D.C.’s tobacco control office has been shut for more than a year. After its most prominent antismoking campaign went off the air, calls to 1-800-QUIT-NOW lines have plummeted.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

item.title: For Families With Nonspeaking Autistic Children, a Fierce Debate Over Assisted Spelling
item.description: Popular communication methods for nonspeaking autistic people have ignited a fierce debate over what counts as evidence of hidden cognitive abilities.
item.dc:creator: Azeen Ghorayshi
item.media:credit: Moriah Ratner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kelly Berg, left, assisted Omer Hashmi, 33, who is a nonspeaker with autism, during a spelling session at the Growing Kids Therapy Center in Herndon, Va.

item.title: A Bird’s Brain Holds Clues to the Sounds of Music
item.description: The neurobiologist Erich Jarvis studies the few species capable of speech. He has long hoped to genetically engineer an animal that can make new calls.
item.dc:creator: Jim Robbins

item.title: Stanley M. Gartler, Pioneer in Cancer Research, Dies at 102
item.description: He provided the first convincing evidence that cancerous tumors develop from a single mutated cell and later helped expose contamination by cells taken from Henrietta Lacks.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: A Mission to Save NASA’s Swift Telescope Launches to Orbit
item.description: A refrigerator-size spacecraft will try to grab onto Swift and nudge it to a higher orbit, enabling it to continue observing powerful cosmic explosions.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: An artist’s concept of the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory telescope being ferried to a higher orbit over Earth by Link, a spacecraft from Katalyst Space Technologies.

item.title: E Pluribus Algae: Why Green Pond Scum Is as American as Red, White and Blue
item.description: And why the most patriotic approach to the Reflecting Pool may be to let the algae take over.
item.dc:creator: Alan Burdick
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times

item.title: Has the MAHA Movement Given Up?
item.description: Robert F. Kennedy Jr. and his allies promised public-health libertarianism. The idea couldn’t survive once they took power.
item.dc:creator: David Wallace-Wells
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
